title: Raymarching
description: Raymarching (sphere tracing) renders 3D scenes entirely inside the fragment shader — no meshes, no vertex buffers. Each fragment shoots a ray from the camera, then repeatedly steps forward by the distance returned by an SDF until it hits a surface or exits the scene. The result is a fully 3D scene built from pure mathematics.

theory.key_concept: Sphere tracing exploits the guarantee of an SDF: the value at any point is the radius of the largest empty sphere at that point. It's safe to jump forward exactly that distance without overshooting a surface. This makes sphere tracing much faster than fixed-step raymarching.
theory.ray_setup: From a camera position `ro` and a pixel's UV, compute a normalized ray direction `rd`. For an orthographic-like setup: `rd = normalize(vec3(uv, -1.0))` where uv is centered ([-1,1]). The march loop advances along `ro + t*rd`.
theory.hit_condition: The loop terminates when `d < 0.001` (hit) or `t > MAX_DIST` (miss). On hit, the position `p = ro + t*rd` is the surface point. Computing the gradient of the SDF at p gives the surface normal via the central difference method.
theory.normal_estimation: The surface normal at hit point p is approximated by finite differences: `N = normalize(vec3(sdf(p+e.xyy)-sdf(p-e.xyy), sdf(p+e.yxy)-sdf(p-e.yxy), sdf(p+e.yyx)-sdf(p-e.yyx)))` where e is a tiny epsilon vec2(0.001, 0.0). This avoids analytical normal derivations for each primitive.

challenges.title: March a Sphere
challenges.description: Implement a minimal sphere tracer. Set up a camera ray from (0, 0, 3) looking toward -Z. March through a sphere SDF centered at the origin with radius 0.8. Output white where the ray hits the sphere and a dark blue background otherwise.
hints: Each iteration, advance `t` by the safe step distance: `t += d`. This is the sphere-tracing guarantee — you can jump forward by exactly the SDF value without crossing a surface.
After the loop, `hit` is true if `d < 0.001` was ever satisfied. Use a ternary to output white for hits and a dark blue for misses.
challenges.unlocks_theory: The `t += d` advance is the entire secret of sphere tracing. A point's SDF value is a guaranteed safe step size — no surface lies closer than that distance. With 64–128 iterations, complex SDF scenes converge to millimeter precision. The `map()` function is where all scene complexity lives; the march loop itself never changes.

hlsl.note: HLSL does not have a `bool` keyword in older profiles — use `int` or `uint` as a flag.

challenges.title: Normals & Diffuse Lighting
challenges.description: Extend the sphere tracer from the previous challenge to compute the surface normal at the hit point using the central-difference method, then apply Lambertian diffuse lighting. Add a floor plane (y = -0.9 SDF) to ground the sphere.
hints: Call `calcNormal(p)` on the hit position. The central-difference normal computes the SDF gradient by evaluating `map` at 6 offset positions — this works for any SDF scene regardless of primitive complexity.
`diff = max(0.0, dot(N, lightDir))` gives Lambertian shading. Add 0.1 ambient and multiply by the surface color.
challenges.unlocks_theory: The central-difference normal estimator is universal: it works for any SDF, including smooth-blended metaballs, domain-warped noise terrains, and boolean CSG scenes. You never have to derive an analytical normal for a new primitive — just call `calcNormal`. The cost is 6 extra SDF evaluations, which is acceptable given that each is cheap arithmetic.

challenges.title: Combined SDF Scene with Soft Shadows
challenges.description: Build a scene with two spheres and a floor plane, combined with `min`. Add an animated rotation to one sphere using `u_time`. Then implement approximate soft shadows by marching a secondary ray from the hit point toward the light and accumulating the minimum SDF value along the way.
hints: Soft shadow: `shadow = min(shadow, k * d / t)`. As the secondary ray approaches an occluder (small d), this ratio drops, softening the shadow edge. Larger k gives harder shadows.
Offset the shadow ray origin by `N * 0.002` to avoid self-intersection artifacts (surface acne).
challenges.unlocks_theory: Soft shadows via secondary march rays are one of raymarching's greatest advantages over rasterization — they're free to add and require no shadow maps, cascades, or bias tuning. The soft shadow estimator by Inigo Quilez (the `k*d/t` formula) became a standard technique for real-time SDF renderers. Penumbra width is controlled entirely by k.
